actions.thought: Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand in heads-up play. Being on the button (Small Blind), I want to raise to take control of the hand and apply pressure. A standard 3x big blind raise to 300 is a solid opening move.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 4d, facing a 3bb (300) open from the Button. In Heads-Up play, while ranges are wide, 6s 4d is a bottom-tier hand (unsuited, low, one-gapper). The opponent's sizing of 3bb offers me pot odds of roughly 33% (calling 200 to win 600), but 64o will struggle to realize its equity out of position, especially against a larger sizing. It is not worth defending this hand against a 3x raise. The prudent play is to fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button (Small Blind) acts first pre-flop and plays in position post-flop. Standard strategy dictates opening a very wide range of hands (often 80%+) to steal the blinds and leverage positional advantage. T5s (Ten-Five suited), while marginal, falls within this opening range due to its suited potential and the ability to represent strength in position. With effective stacks near 100BB, a min-raise is a low-risk play to define the range and potentially take the pot down immediately or navigate post-flop with position.

J2o is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind. While the pot odds are 3:1 against a 2x open, J2o has poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. It is better to wait for a stronger range to engage in a pot.

I am on the button with 4s 3s, which is a speculative hand but has decent playability in a heads-up situation. Raising allows me to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop, similar to Hand #1. A 3x raise is a standard opening size to put pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd Jc. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button/SB. QJo is a strong hand in heads-up play, well within the top range of hands to defend. While a 3-bet is an option to seize initiative, calling is the standard play to keep the pot manageable while playing out of position post-flop against a likely wide opening range. The price is 200 to win a pot that will be 600, giving me 2:1 odds which is sufficient for QJo.

On the flop of 7c 9c 2d, I hold Qd Jc (two overcards). As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Donk betting here is unnecessary as I have no strong draw or made hand to protect, and checking allows me to see what the opponent does. If they check back, I get to see a free turn. If they bet, I can re-evaluate based on sizing.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop (7c 9c 2d) is relatively disconnected, though it could hit some of the Big Blind's defending range. Since Pro checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of about 1/3 the pot to represent a strong range and try to take the pot down immediately. If called, I have very little equity, but the bet puts pressure on missed overcards.

I am facing a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600 (33% pot). With QdJc on a 7c9c2d board, I have two overcards and the Jack of clubs, giving me decent equity against a wide heads-up opening range. The price requires me to have only about 20% equity to continue. Folding here would be over-folding against a standard small c-bet frequency. Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily when I have showdown value and outs. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn, especially if I hit a Queen, Jack, Ten, or Eight.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades improves my hand to Top Pair with a Queen kicker (QdJc on 7c9c2dJs). This is a strong hand in a heads-up situation. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor who also bet the flop. Checking accomplishes two things: 1) It keeps the opponent's bluffing range wide, allowing them to barrel with draws (flush or straight draws) or air. 2) It controls the pot size. If I lead out (donk bet), I likely fold out their bluffs and only get action from hands that beat me or strong draws. I plan to call a bet if the opponent fires again.

The Jack of Spades on the turn is a good card to continue my aggression as a double barrel. It is an overcard to most of the flop and fits well into my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Since I have no showdown value with 4-high, I will bet around 60% of the pot to put pressure on my opponent's marginal made hands like a pair of 7s or 9s, or a draw that might fold to continued pressure.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades gives me Top Pair with a Queen kicker. The opponent bets 600 into a pot of 1000 (60% pot). My hand is very strong in a heads-up scenario, beating all semi-bluffs (club draws, straight draws like QT/T9) and weaker made hands (JT, 9x). However, raising here would likely isolate me against hands that beat me (AJ, KJ, Sets, Two Pair, or the T8 straight) while folding out the bluffs I want to keep in. The price to call requires roughly 27% equity, which I easily possess. Calling controls the pot size and allows me to evaluate the river, while giving the opponent a chance to bluff again on the next street.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (QdJc) on a board of 7c 9c 2d Js 3h. The river 3h is a blank that completes no draws. The flush draw from the flop has missed. I am out of position. Leading out (donk betting) here is not optimal because worse hands (missed draws, lower pairs) will likely fold, and better hands (AJ, KJ, Sets, Two Pair) will call or raise. By checking, I give the opponent the opportunity to bluff with their missed club draws or straight draws, or to value bet a worse Jack. If they check back, I have enough showdown value to win a fair share of pots. Therefore, checking is the standard play to maximize value from bluffs and minimize loss against superior hands.

I have bottom pair (3s) on a board of 7-9-2-J-3. I have been the aggressor throughout the hand, but Pro has called every bet so far. While a third barrel on the river could potentially force a fold from a weak pair of 7s or 9s, the river 3h actually gives me some marginal showdown value against busted club draws or straight draws like 8-10. Since Pro has shown stickiness by calling the flop and turn, I will check back to see the showdown and potentially win the pot without risking more chips against a hand that likely won't fold.

In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong holding. 55 is vulnerable to overcards but has good equity against a random hand. Being on the button (SB), I want to exercise positional advantage and initiative. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength. Limping allows the Big Blind to see a free flop or raise aggressively. A standard min-raise to 200 allows me to build the pot, potentially take it down pre-flop, or play in position with the lead.

I have Ace-Seven offsuit in the Big Blind. Pro opened for a minimum raise to 200. Given the 4:1 pot odds (100 to call into a 300 pot) and the strength of Ace-high in heads-up play, this is a mandatory defense. I will call to see the flop and keep the pot size manageable while out of position.

I am out of position on a board that significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro's range likely includes many broadway cards like Jack-Ten, Queen-Jack, etc.). My Ace-Seven has missed the flop entirely. The standard play here is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) into this board texture is risky and unlikely to succeed against a strong range. I will check and evaluate based on Pro's action.

I have flopped a set of fives on a coordinated board (Js Tc 5c). While this is a very strong hand, the board texture is wet with flush draws (clubs) and straight connectors (8-9, Q-K, etc.) present. Checking back is risky because many turn cards could either complete an opponent's draw or kill the action. I need to build the pot and charge draws while getting value from top pairs like Jx or Tx. A half-pot bet of 200 extracts value from worse hands and offers incorrect odds to draws without folding out hands like middle pair.

I am facing a 1/2 pot continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot (total pot now 600). The board of Tc-Js-5c is very dynamic and hits the pre-flop raiser's range hard, containing many broadways and flush draws. While I have Ace-high, I have very little equity on this board and no backdoor draws other than a runner-runner straight or trips. Calling out of position against a strong opponent with just Ace-high and no real plan for future streets on such a connected board is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In heads-up play, maintaining button aggression is key. While 52s is a weak hand, it is suited and can occasionally flop well. I have established a 3x opening size in previous orbits, and I will continue with that here to put pressure on the big blind and maintain a balanced opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6o facing a 3x open (300 total) from the Button. While Q6o has reasonable raw equity against a wide heads-up opening range, the larger raise size (3bb vs 2bb) reduces my pot odds, requiring about 33% equity realization out of position. Q6o plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated by better Queens or Ace-highs. Given the opponent's tendency to open larger, tightening up my defense range slightly is prudent to avoid bleeding chips in difficult spots. I fold.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on the Button (Small Blind), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, well ahead of a random range. My opponent (Flash) has shown a willingness to fold to aggression post-flop in previous hands. Raising pre-flop allows me to take the initiative, build the pot with a strong hand, and deny free equity to the Big Blind. A standard min-raise to 200 is appropriate to balance my range and keeps the pot manageable relative to the stack sizes.

I am in the Big Blind with Q9o. The opponent (Button) has raised to 200, which is a standard 2x open. I need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot of 300. My hand has plenty of equity against a standard heads-up opening range, and the pot odds (25%) make this an easy defend. I will call and see a flop.

I have middle pair (nines) with a Queen kicker on a monotone board of clubs. This is a very coordinated and dangerous texture, especially since I do not hold a club. Checking is the standard play here as the out-of-position defender to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any club or overcard, so I prefer to keep the pot small.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (Tens) plus the Nut Flush Draw (Ac) on a monotone board (Tc 9c 6c). This is a very strong hand, but the board texture is wet and dangerous. By holding the Ace of Clubs, I block the nut flush, meaning my opponent cannot have the nuts. Checking back is optimal here for several reasons: 1) It exercises pot control, preventing the pot from getting too large on a volatile board. 2) It disguises the strength of my hand. 3) It induces bluffs from my opponent on later streets. 4) Since I have the nut flush draw, giving a 'free card' isn't as dangerous because I have the best draw and top pair. If my opponent has a made flush, I want to minimize loss or hit my flush cheaply. If he has air, he folds to a bet anyway. Checking keeps his range wide.

The turn brings a fourth club to the board, which is a very dangerous card for me as I do not hold a club. My pair of nines is now strictly a bluff-catcher. Since the flop was checked by both players, it is possible the opponent doesn't have a high club, but any club at all now beats me. I will check to see how the opponent reacts; betting would likely only be called by better hands (any club).

I hold the absolute nuts with the Ace of Clubs on a board showing four clubs (Tc 9c 6c 3c). My opponent has checked to me. On this texture, it is extremely difficult for my opponent to call a bet unless they have a Club (making a lower flush). Hands without a club are drawing dead or nearly dead and will fold to any bet. By checking back, I accomplish two things: 1) I induce bluffs on the river from opponents holding 'air' or pairs who perceive my double-check as weakness. 2) I disguise the strength of my hand, potentially leading a lower flush to value bet the river or comfortably call a river bet. Betting now likely only isolates me against strong hands or forces folds from the hands I want to keep in the pot.

The board is extremely coordinated with four clubs and a paired Ten. I have two pair (Tens and Nines), but any single club in the opponent's hand or a Ten beats me. Given that the flop and turn were checked through by both players, it is unlikely the opponent has a high flush or a very strong hand, but even a small club or a six is problematic. Betting here doesn't serve much purpose as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I won't fold out better hands. Checking allows me to reach showdown and potentially win against a hand with no clubs or a lower pair.

I hold the absolute nuts with the Ace-high flush on a board of [Tc 9c 6c 3c Td]. Since I am in position (Button/SB) and the opponent has checked to me, checking back would end the hand immediately, gaining zero additional chips. The logic in the previous thoughts suggesting checking to 'induce bluffs' is incorrect for the river in position, as there are no future streets. I must bet to extract value from worse hands such as Trips (Tx) or lower flushes (Kc, Qc, etc.) that might call given my passive line on the Flop and Turn. A half-pot bet of 200 is small enough to get called by these marginal hands while maximizing my stack increase.

The board is highly coordinated with four clubs and a paired Ten. I have two pair, Tens and Nines. The opponent's betting line (raising pre-flop, then checking both the flop and the turn) suggests they likely do not have a strong flush or were playing very cautiously. The river bet of 200 into a 400 pot (making it 200 to call for a total pot of 800) provides me with 3:1 pot odds. This means I only need to win 25% of the time to break even. The bet could represent a Ten that was played slowly, a small club, or a total bluff trying to take down a pot that has been checked through. Given the price and the fact that most strong hands would have bet earlier to protect against the fourth club or build a pot, I will call to catch potential bluffs or weak value bets.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Jack-Deuce offsuit. While I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand, J2o is a very weak starting hand that doesn't flop well. Given that I have already established an aggressive raising image from the button in previous hands (Hand #1 and Hand #5), I can afford to fold the bottom of my range here to maintain a balanced profile and avoid playing a weak hand out of control against 'Pro'.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button (Small Blind) generally opens a very wide range of hands (often 80%+). However, J5 offsuit is near the very bottom of the starting hand range (bottom 35%). While I have position post-flop, the hand has poor playability due to the lack of connectivity and the low kicker. My opponent has defended their Big Blind 2 out of 3 times against raises so far (Hands 4 and 6), suggesting they are not over-folding to steals enough to justify opening any two cards. Given my chip lead, there is no need to force the action with trash hands. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a top-tier starting hand in heads-up play. My previous strategy involves opening to 300 (3x the big blind) from the small blind to build the pot and take control of the hand. Given the strength of my hole cards, I will continue with this aggressive approach to extract value and potentially end the hand pre-flop or set up a strong post-flop scenario.

Opponent has raised to 300 (3BB) from the button. I hold 9c 2c in the Big Blind. While suited, this is one of the weakest hands in the deck. Being out of position against a 3x raise makes it difficult to realize equity. Although I am getting 2:1 pot odds, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability make this a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Qs5s, a suited Queen. In heads-up play, the button should open a wide range of hands (typically 70%+) to steal the blinds and utilize positional advantage post-flop. My opponent (Flash) has defended his Big Blind by calling min-raises in previous hands but has not yet 3-bet from the BB, suggesting he is somewhat passive pre-flop when out of position. Q5s is strong enough to play for a raise rather than a limp or fold. A min-raise to 200 is the standard sizing to apply pressure while keeping the risk low.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s3c. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (100 more to win a total pot of 300), 93o is an extremely weak hand that realizes equity poorly out of position. Against a competent opponent like Pro, defending such a wide range of trash hands will likely lead to significant losses in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with T8o, which is a solid hand to open in a heads-up scenario. Looking at previous hands, raising to 300 from the small blind has been effective in picking up the blinds or establishing control. I will continue this pattern to maintain aggression.

I hold a premium hand (AQo) in the Big Blind. In a Heads-Up match, this is a monster hand that dominates a significant portion of the Button's opening range (Ax, Kx, Qx, and connectors). The opponent has opened for 3bb (300). While a 3-bet is a valid option to build the pot for value, I will be out of position (OOP) post-flop. Flat calling is an excellent strategy here for several reasons: 1) It keeps the opponent's range wide, including hands I dominate; 2) It controls the pot size while playing OOP; 3) It disguises the strength of my hand. Given the deep stacks (81bb effective), there is plenty of room to maneuver post-flop. I will call the additional 200 to see a flop.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a board of Qh 3s 4h. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including bluffs (missed overcards), semi-bluffs (flush draws, straight draws), or worse value hands (KQ, QJ, mid-pairs). Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their air and only get action from hands that have good equity or beat me. I plan to check-call or check-raise depending on the bet size, but checking first is the correct move to maximize value.

The flop comes Qh-3s-4h. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture favors my perceived range, as a Queen is more likely to be in my raising range than the defender's calling range. Since Pro checked, I will fire a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to represent strength and attempt to take the pot down immediately, or at least define where I stand in the hand.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AQ) on a Q-3-4 board with two hearts. My opponent has bet 300 (half-pot). While a check-raise is a valid option to extract value from draws (hearts, straight draws) and worse Queens, it risks folding out the opponent's bluffs and isolating me against a very strong range (sets, two pair). Being out of position, check-calling is the prudent play. It keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing on future streets or value-own themselves with worse top pairs (KQ, QJ). I am well ahead of their range and want to keep the pot manageable while extracting value across multiple streets rather than forcing the action now. I call the 300.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AQ) on a board of Qh 3s 4h 4c. The turn card pairs the board with the 4c, which is generally a safe card for my hand unless the opponent has a 4 (unlikely given the pre-flop 3bb raise) or a pocket pair like 33/44. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who bet the flop. Standard play is to check to the raiser again. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs and weaker hands while isolating me against better hands or strong draws. Checking gives the opponent the opportunity to bluff at the paired board or bet worse value hands (like KQ/QJ). I plan to call a reasonable bet.

The 4 of clubs on the turn is a relative blank that pairs the bottom card of the flop. While I represent a strong range as the pre-flop aggressor, Pro's call on the flop suggests they have a piece of this board, such as a Queen, a pocket pair, or a heart draw. Given that my hand (Ten-high) has no current showdown value and no draws, I will check back to control the pot size. This allows me to see a river card for free and decide whether to bluff or give up based on the final board state and Pro's next action.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AQ) on a board of Qh 3s 4h 4c 9d. The river 9d is a safe card. The key information is the opponent's check-back on the turn. This action typically caps their range, suggesting they hold a marginal made hand (like a weak Queen, middle pocket pair JJ-55) that wants a cheap showdown, or a complete air hand that gave up. It is unlikely they hold a strong hand (sets, strong Q) or a strong draw, as an aggressive player like Flash would usually continue betting the turn. 

Being out of position on the river, I have two options: check to induce bluffs or bet for value. Since the turn check indicates a lack of aggression/draws, checking will likely result in the opponent checking back with their showdown value hands (SDV). These SDV hands (like 88, 99, TT, JJ) will rarely bet themselves but might make a 'crying call' against a reasonable bet. To extract the maximum value from this capped range, I should lead out. A large bet might fold out the mid-pairs I'm targeting, so a smaller value bet of roughly 1/3 to 1/2 pot is optimal to induce a call from worse hands. I will bet 500 into the 1200 pot.

I have Ten-high with no showdown value. After I checked back the turn, Pro led out for 500 into a 1200 pot on a 9-high river. While the bet sizing is relatively small, Pro's check-call on the flop suggests they have at least a piece of the board, such as a Queen, a small pair, or perhaps a Nine that just improved. Since I cannot win this hand at showdown and a bluff-raise is unlikely to succeed against a range that already called a flop bet, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

In Heads-Up play, Queen-Seven suited (Q7s) is a strong enough hand to open from the Button (Small Blind). It has both high-card value and flush potential. My opponent (Flash) has been playing reasonably standard but hasn't shown significant 3-betting aggression yet. Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to take the initiative, build the pot in position, and potentially steal the blinds immediately. This is a standard value/steal raise in this configuration.

I have been dealt pocket nines (9s 9c), which is a very strong hand in a heads-up match. Pro has raised from the button to 200. While I could just call to keep the pot smaller out of position, 99 is well ahead of most of Pro's button opening range. I will 3-bet to 600 to build a pot, take initiative, and potentially end the hand right now. If Pro calls, I have a strong overpair on many low boards.

Facing a 3-bet to 600, I have to call 400 more into a pot of 800, offering 2:1 pot odds (33% equity required). In Heads-Up play, the Button (Small Blind) acts last post-flop, granting me a significant positional advantage. Q7s is a playable hand with flush potential and high-card value. Against a typical 3-bet range that includes bluffs and value, Q7s has sufficient equity to call, especially given the positional edge. Folding is too tight for this price, and 4-betting turns a playable hand into a bluff unnecessarily.

The flop of Ts-As-Js is extremely coordinated and connects heavily with the range of hands that would call a 3-bet pre-flop. While I have the 9s, giving me a flush draw, my pocket nines are now an underpair to all three cards on the board. Since I am out of position and the board texture favors many of the hands in Pro's calling range (any Ace, Jack, Ten, or Broadway straight draws), I will check to see how the opponent reacts. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially realize my equity if the turn is favorable.

I hold Qh7h on a monotone broadway flop of AsJsTs. The pot is 1200 after I called a pre-flop 3-bet. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. This board heavily favors the 3-bettor's range (sets of Aces/Jacks/Tens, straights with KQ, flushes, or strong top pairs). I have no spades and only Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). Betting here is unlikely to fold out any hand that beats me (like Ax or a spade draw) and risks getting check-raised. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, trying to hit a King on the turn or get to showdown cheaply if the board runs out favorably. It is a clear spot to check back given the dangerous texture and my lack of immediate strength.

The 3c on the turn is a complete brick and does not change the board texture. My pocket nines remain an underpair to the board, but I still hold the 9s for a flush draw. Since the flop went check-check, Pro's range may be somewhat capped, but this board still heavily favors the broadway cards they would call a 3-bet with. I will check again to control the pot size and look to realize my equity. Betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands (like an Ace or a Jack) and I have some marginal showdown value if the hand remains checked down.

The turn is the 3c, a complete brick that does not change the board texture (As Js Ts). The board remains very wet with a monotone broadway texture. My opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop, has now checked twice. While this shows weakness, it could easily be a trap with a flush or pot control with a hand like Ax or a high pocket pair scared of the flush. I hold Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). Betting here turns my hand into a pure bluff that is unlikely to fold out top pair (Ax) or a flush draw. By checking back, I realize my equity for free, giving myself a chance to hit a King on the river to make a straight. There is no value in betting Q-high here given the risk of being check-raised or called by better.

The river 6s completes a four-flush on the board. I have the 9s, giving me a 9-high flush. Since the flop and turn were checked by both players, the pot remains relatively small. While a flush is a strong hand, the board of As Ts Js 6s means any player with the Ks or Qs has a higher flush. By checking, I can evaluate the opponent's action; I don't want to bet and get raised by a superior flush, and checking allows me to potentially catch a bluff or reach a cheap showdown with a hand that has significant value.

On the River, the board reads Ts As Js 3c 6s, putting four spades on the board. I hold Qh 7h, which gives me Queen-high and zero showdown value; checking back guarantees a loss against any pair or any spade. My opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked all three streets (flop, turn, and river). This passive line on a wet, monotone board strongly suggests they do not hold a strong flush or a strong made hand, as those would likely bet for value or protection. They are likely capped at a one-pair hand (like Ax or KK/QQ without a spade) or a missed draw. Given the scary 4-flush board texture, it is extremely difficult for a non-spade hand to call a significant bet. My line of checking back the flop and turn is consistent with a flush draw that has now arrived or a pot-controlled made flush. Therefore, I will turn my hand into a bluff and bet approximately 60% of the pot to force a fold from better non-flush hands.

On a board of As Ts Js 3c 6s, there are four spades. I hold the 9s, giving me a 9-high flush. While the Ks and Qs would make higher flushes, the action has been very passive (checked through flop and turn), making it less likely that Pro is holding the absolute nuts and trying to trap. After I checked the river, Pro bet 750 into a 1200 pot. In heads-up play, a flush is too strong to fold in this scenario, especially given that Pro could be bluffing with a non-spade hand or value-betting a lower spade or a set/straight. I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button (SB) with King-Five suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Historically, I have been opening to 300 (3x the big blind) from this position and it has been effective in picking up the blinds or establishing control. I will continue with this standard raise sizing to maintain a consistent profile and apply pressure on the opponent.

I hold 4h 2c in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker due to being unsuited, low, and disconnected. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB). I would need to call 200 more chips into a pot that will be 600, giving me 33% pot odds. My equity with 42o against a standard button raising range is typically less than 30%, and playing this hand out of position is difficult and likely to realize even less equity. Therefore, folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 5c 7h, which is a weak off-suit one-gapper. In heads-up play, while I want to play a wide range of hands (typically 80%+), 75o sits near the bottom of the starting hand rankings (around the bottom 20%). I previously folded J5o on the button (Hand #8), which has slightly better raw equity than 75o. My opponent 'Flash' has shown willingness to defend the Big Blind by calling (Hands #4, #6) and even 3-betting (Hand #12). Opening with such a weak hand risks bleeding chips against an active opponent. Folding here is a disciplined play to maintain a solid range.

I am on the button in a heads-up match with King-Five offsuit. This hand is well within the standard opening range for this position. Looking at the history, opening for 3x (300 chips) has been my consistent sizing and has successfully forced folds or allowed me to take control of the pot. I will continue with a standard raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with [5h Js] facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. The pot odds are 200 to call into a total pot of 600, requiring roughly 33% equity. While J5o has decent raw equity against a wide heads-up range, it suffers from poor playability and realization of equity out of position, especially against a larger open size. I have folded to this sizing multiple times, but J5o is below the standard defending threshold for a 3bb open. Defending here would likely lead to a negative expected value situation due to the difficulty of playing weak offsuit hands post-flop without initiative. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct play.

I hold 3h Tc on the button (SB). This is a statistically weak hand (bottom 25% of hands), even for heads-up play. While being in position suggests aggressive opening ranges, T3o is generally below the threshold for a profitable open-raise or limp against a competent opponent who defends their big blind. My opponent has shown willingness to defend and 3-bet, making a steal attempt with this trash hand negative EV. I will fold and preserve my chip lead for better opportunities.

Holding Ace-Four offsuit (A4o) on the button in a heads-up game is a strong starting hand, well within the top range of hands that should be played aggressively. My established pattern in this tournament has been to open for 300 (a 3x raise) from the Small Blind, which has been successful in picking up several pots pre-flop. I will continue this strategy to apply pressure on the Big Blind and maintain my table image.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. The pot odds require 33% equity to call (200 to win 600). I have folded hands like Q6o to similar raises previously. Without a read that the opponent plays poorly post-flop or opens 100% of hands, folding K6o to a 3BB raise is the disciplined play to preserve chips and avoid difficult post-flop spots.

I am on the Button (SB) with a strong heads-up hand, Queen-Ten suited (Qd Td). In heads-up poker, the Button acts first pre-flop but has position (acts last) on all subsequent streets. QTs is well within the top range of hands to open-raise for value. My standard opening size in this match has been a min-raise to 200, which balances well with my entire opening range. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a pot in position with a hand that has good playability post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h7h, a strong suited connector that plays well in heads-up situations. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200, which is a standard 2x open from the Small Blind/Button. I have a very strong drawing hand with a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (12 total outs). Acting first on the flop, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to gauge their hand strength based on their betting action. If they bet, I can choose to check-call to realize my equity or check-raise as a semi-bluff to put pressure on their range.

I hold Top Pair (Tens) with a strong kicker (Queen) on a board of 6h-Th-4s. This is a strong value hand in a heads-up pot. Therefore, I should bet for value and protection rather than checking back. A bet targets worse Tens (JT, T9), mid-pairs (77-99), lower pairs, and flush/straight draws. A half-pot sizing (200) is standard to charge draws while keeping the opponent's calling range wide enough to include worse hands.

I have a very strong semi-bluffing hand with a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, giving me 12 outs to likely the best hand. The opponent has made a half-pot continuation bet of 200. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, and my equity to hit one of my draws on the turn is approximately 25.5%, which makes calling mathematically correct even without considering implied odds. While a check-raise is a strong option to apply pressure, calling allows me to realize my equity more cheaply and keeps the pot manageable if I miss the turn, while still allowing me to extract value if I hit.

I currently hold a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (to an eight), giving me approximately 12 outs to improve to a very strong hand. The deuce of diamonds on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Being out of position, I will continue with the strategy of checking to the aggressor. This allows me to potentially see a free river card if the opponent checks behind, or to evaluate their bet sizing if they choose to continue their aggression. Given my high equity, I intend to see the river card.

I hold Top Pair with a good kicker (QTs) on a board of 6h-Th-4s-2d. The turn card (2d) is a complete blank that does not complete any draws or likely improve the opponent's range. A bet of half-pot (400) is consistent with my flop sizing, offers a good price for worse hands to call, and denies free equity to draws. If I check, I risk an overcard or flush card killing my action or beating me on the river.

I have a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, totaling 12 outs. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring 25% equity to call. With 12 outs, I have approximately 26% equity to hit on the river. Given the decent price and the potential for implied odds if I hit, calling is the most profitable and standard play here to see the river.

On the river, the board is 6h-Th-4s-2d-9d. I have pair of nines (9h 7h). The flush draw and the gutshot missed. I checked, and my opponent (Pro) also checked behind. I hold Top Pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker on a board of 6h-Th-4s-2d-9d. I have bet for value on both the flop and turn, extracting chips from draws and worse pairs. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value against missed draws and weaker pairs without risking being raised or value-owning myself against improved hands.

I have Ac 6c on the button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, offering both top-pair potential and nut-flush possibilities. I have 8-high on a T-5-A board after calling a pre-flop raise. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, specifically with the Ace and Ten. I hold King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Deuce for the wheel) on an A-T-5-4 board. The opponent leads for half-pot on the turn after the flop was checked through. I am on the button with A5o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play (approximately top 30%). I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Six of diamonds. Pro has raised to 200 from the small blind. The pot is currently 300, and it costs me only 100 more to call, giving me 3:1 pot odds. I hold Ace-high (Ac5h) on a board of 9s 2c 3s 3h. After checking back the flop, the turn pairs the board with the 3h. My opponent checks to me again. I hold Ace-high (A-T-9-3-3) on a board of 9s 2c 3s 3h Tc. The pot is 400. The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn, and my opponent checked the river. I have T8s on the button, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play with high playability. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to put pressure on the big blind and build a pot with a hand that flops well. I'll make it 3x the big blind, which has been my consistent sizing.

I defended my Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. The flop comes Q-7-3 with two clubs. I hold Jc Td on an Ac 9c 6h board. As the pre-flop raiser, the Ace hits my perceived range well, but it also connects with the Big Blind's calling range. I hold Jack-high on an Ac 9c 6h 5s board. After checking back the flop, the turn card (5s) is relatively safe but doesn't improve my hand. I have King-high (Kh 8h) on a board of Ac 9c 6h 5s Th. King-Jack of clubs is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Consistent with my previous opening strategy, I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand from the button.

I have Queen-high on a Jack-high board after calling a pre-flop raise. I've missed the flop completely. My opponent (Pro) has position on me and initiated the action. The turn card is a 7c, pairing the board. The board is now J-7-3-7 rainbow. My hand, A9o, still holds significant showdown value as Ace-high. On the River, the board is J-7-3-7-6. I hold Ace-high (A9). The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn, and the opponent has checked again on the river.
